title: Treacle scones
subtitle: Scottish scones with black treacle and warm spice
excerpt: Scottish treacle scones: a darker, spiced scone enriched with black treacle, which gives colour, depth, and a slight bitterness that balances the sugar. Denser and more flavourful than a plain scone.
sourceNotes: Treacle scones are a traditional Scottish bake, particularly associated with bakeries in the Central Belt. They appear in Scottish household cookery guides from the late 19th and early 20th centuries and are documented in period regional collections as a standard accompaniment to tea. The use of black treacle (molasses) for colour and flavour, and bicarbonate of soda as the leavening agent rather than baking powder, are characteristic of the older Scottish form.

content.text: Treacle scones are darker and more flavourful than plain scones. The black treacle works with bicarbonate of soda as leavening: the treacle is acidic, the bicarb is alkaline, and the reaction produces the rise. The resulting
is stickier than a plain scone dough; handle it lightly.

Preheat the oven to 220°C conventional. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Sift the flour, bicarbonate of soda, mixed spice, salt, and sugar into a bowl. Rub in the cold butter until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs. Stir the treacle into the warm milk until dissolved. Pour into the flour mixture and stir quickly until a soft, slightly sticky dough forms. Turn onto a lightly floured surface, pat gently to 2 cm thick, and cut 8 rounds with a floured 5 cm cutter. Place on the tray. Bake for 12 to 14 minutes until risen and the tops feel firm. Transfer to a rack.
